Navigation designed around experience, not efficiency.
What if navigation optimised for how you feel, not just where you go? Reimagining walking as an experience with routing for safety, discovery, and human-ness.

## The Question

Every navigation app solves the same problem: getting you there fast. (re)Route solves a different one — making the walk worth taking.

## How It Works

You open the app. You type what you actually want: "calm 20 min walk," "artsy date in Born," "take me to Park Güell but make it scenic," or just "surprise me." A single AI call interprets your words — not as a search query, but as an intent — and builds a walk around it.

### Three Entry Points, Three Mindsets

**GO** — destination search with route options that explain *why* each route is different, not just how long it takes.

**EXPLORE** — type a vibe, the AI curates an experience. From a themed architecture trail to a multi-stop evening out.

**WANDER** — a compass, curated pins around you, and nothing else. No route. No destination. Just direction.

## The Data Underneath

119,000 Barcelona street segments scored across noise, greenery, cleanliness, cultural richness, and safety. When you ask for "calm," the system knows which streets are actually quiet. After dark, every route automatically shifts to well-lit, active streets. Safety isn't a mode. It's a baseline.

## Smart Discovery

Ask for "artsy date" and the system reasons about what the query implies — experimental galleries, design bookshops, natural wine bars with art on the walls — then verifies every suggestion against real place data before showing it.

## Curated Layer

386 hand-curated points of interest. Themed walks spanning Gaudí, Gothic Barcelona, modernisme, hidden courtyards, public art, rooftop views, Roman ruins. A bloom calendar tracks 8 flowering species by month.

## The Research

8 in-depth interviews. 89% of survey respondents already vary their routes to explore. The tools just haven't caught up.

## What This Is

A master's thesis prototype — working proof that navigation can be designed around experience, not just efficiency. Built for Barcelona. Designed for any city worth walking.

A game about noticing the small stuff.
One word. One photo. One day. A shared visual diary of a city. Built through small, daily moments.

## The Insight

We walk past a thousand interesting things every day without seeing them.

## How It Works

A mobile web app that gives everyone in Barcelona the same single-word prompt each day — *texture*, *tiny*, or *bright*. Step outside, find it, snap a photo, drop it on a shared city map. At midnight, the map resets.

No likes, no followers, no algorithm. Just a quiet dare to look more carefully.

## The Reframing

A cracked tile becomes the answer to *pattern*. A stranger's dog becomes *soft*. The shared map turns Barcelona into a collaborative, ever-changing gallery that lives for exactly one day.

## What I Built

Research & concept. Full Figma prototypes. Next.js frontend. Supabase backend. Leaflet mapping. Deployed on Vercel, currently in beta.

## Design Philosophy

Deliberately simple. No gamification layers, no streaks, no social pressure. The constraint of one word and one day creates focus. The shared map creates connection. Anti-social media — using a phone to make you look up from it.

Trip planning that looks as good as the trip.
A planning tool that thinks like an editor, not a spreadsheet. Live weather, image search, and inline editing — all in one flow.

## The Problem

Trip planning tools are either ugly spreadsheets or rigid templates. I wanted something useful for planning — editable, data-connected — that also looks like something you'd want to look at.

## What It Does

A 15-day Europe Alps Tour planned and displayed in an editorial layout inspired by travel magazines. Click-to-edit everything. Live weather per destination. Built-in image search. Asymmetric photo galleries.

## Design Approach

The layout reproduces a Keynote presentation I designed — city intro slides with split typography, day pages with photo galleries and timelines, weather widgets. A planning tool that's also beautiful enough to screenshot and share.

## What I Learned

My first real frontend project built with Claude Code — I designed the visual system, wrote the data architecture and specification, then used AI-assisted development to build it. The process itself became proof of concept for how a designer-strategist can ship functional products.
